name: Lambda Development
description: Serverless function development including handlers, event sources, and execution environment
key_facts: Handler function receives event object and context object
Max execution timeout: 15 minutes, max memory: 10 GB
Environment variables for configuration (encrypted with KMS)
Layers for sharing code and libraries across functions
Concurrency limit: 1000 per region by default, reserved concurrency per function
common_misconceptions: Lambda functions maintain state between invocations (each invocation may use a new or reused execution environment)
Increasing memory only increases RAM (it also proportionally increases CPU and network bandwidth)

name: API Gateway
description: Managed API service for creating REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs
key_facts: REST API: full-featured with caching, request validation, WAF integration
HTTP API: lower cost, lower latency, simpler feature set
Stages allow deploying different versions (dev, staging, prod)
Stage variables act as environment variables for each stage
Usage plans and API keys for throttling and metering third-party access
common_misconceptions: REST API and HTTP API are the same (HTTP API is cheaper but lacks some REST API features like caching and request validation)
API Gateway has no request limits (default throttle is 10,000 requests per second with 5,000 burst)

name: DynamoDB for Developers
description: DynamoDB data modeling, queries, and SDK usage for application development
key_facts: Partition key (hash key) determines data distribution; sort key enables range queries
Query operation retrieves items by partition key; Scan reads the entire table
Global Secondary Index (GSI): different partition and sort key, eventually consistent
Local Secondary Index (LSI): same partition key, different sort key, must be created at table creation
Conditional writes prevent race conditions (optimistic locking with version numbers)
common_misconceptions: Scan is efficient for large tables (Scan reads every item and is expensive; use Query with proper key design)
GSIs can be strongly consistent (GSIs only support eventually consistent reads)

name: SQS and SNS
description: Message queuing and publish/subscribe notification services for decoupled architectures
key_facts: SQS Standard: at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering, unlimited throughput
SQS FIFO: exactly-once processing, guaranteed ordering, 3000 msg/s with batching
Visibility timeout prevents other consumers from processing a message while it is being processed
Dead-letter queues capture messages that fail processing after maxReceiveCount
SNS supports fan-out to multiple SQS queues, Lambda, HTTP, email, and SMS
common_misconceptions: SQS guarantees message ordering by default (only FIFO queues guarantee ordering; Standard queues use best-effort)
SNS stores messages for later delivery (SNS delivers immediately; if subscriber is down, the message is lost unless using SQS)

name: Step Functions and X-Ray
description: Serverless workflow orchestration and distributed tracing
key_facts: Standard workflows: long-running (up to 1 year), exactly-once execution
Express workflows: short-duration (up to 5 minutes), at-least-once, higher throughput
State types: Task, Choice, Parallel, Wait, Map, Pass, Succeed, Fail
X-Ray provides end-to-end tracing of requests across distributed services
X-Ray daemon collects trace segments and sends them to the X-Ray service
common_misconceptions: Standard and Express workflows have the same pricing model (Standard charges per state transition, Express charges per execution duration)
X-Ray traces every request by default (sampling rules control what percentage of requests are traced)

name: Amazon Cognito
description: User authentication and authorization for web and mobile applications
key_facts: User Pools: user directory for sign-up, sign-in, and token management (JWT tokens)
Identity Pools: provide temporary AWS credentials for accessing AWS services directly
User Pools return ID token, access token, and refresh token
Supports federation with social providers (Google, Facebook, Apple) and SAML/OIDC
User Pools can trigger Lambda functions at various authentication stages
common_misconceptions: User Pools and Identity Pools do the same thing (User Pools authenticate, Identity Pools authorize AWS access)
Cognito tokens last indefinitely (ID and access tokens expire after 1 hour by default; refresh tokens are configurable)

name: IAM for Developers
description: IAM roles, policies, and STS for application-level access control
key_facts: EC2 instance profiles provide temporary credentials to applications running on EC2
STS AssumeRole provides temporary credentials for cross-account access
Policy conditions: aws:SourceIp, aws:RequestedRegion, aws:PrincipalTag
IAM policy simulator tests policies before deploying them
Never embed access keys in code; use IAM roles and environment variables
common_misconceptions: Access keys in environment variables are the best practice (IAM roles via instance profiles or ECS task roles are preferred)
STS temporary credentials never expire (they have a configurable duration, default 1 hour)

name: KMS and Secrets Manager
description: Encryption key management and secure secrets storage for applications
key_facts: KMS Encrypt API: encrypt up to 4 KB of data directly
Envelope encryption: use GenerateDataKey to get a plaintext and encrypted data key for larger data
Secrets Manager stores and automatically rotates database credentials and API keys
Secrets Manager uses Lambda functions for automatic secret rotation
SSM Parameter Store: free for standard parameters, supports SecureString (KMS-encrypted)
common_misconceptions: KMS can encrypt large files directly (KMS Encrypt only handles up to 4 KB; use envelope encryption for larger data)
Parameter Store and Secrets Manager are interchangeable (Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation and cross-account access, Parameter Store does not)

name: STS and Federation
description: Security Token Service for temporary credentials and identity federation
key_facts: AssumeRole: assume a role in the same or different account
AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity: exchange web identity token (Google, Facebook) for AWS credentials
AssumeRoleWithSAML: exchange SAML assertion for AWS credentials (enterprise SSO)
GetSessionToken: MFA-protected API access for IAM users
Temporary credentials include AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, and SessionToken
common_misconceptions: AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity is the recommended way for mobile apps (use Cognito Identity Pools instead, which calls STS behind the scenes)
STS is a regional service that must be explicitly enabled (STS is global by default, with optional regional endpoints)

name: CodePipeline and CI/CD
description: Continuous integration and delivery pipeline service
key_facts: CodeCommit: managed Git repository (source stage)
CodeBuild: managed build service, uses buildspec.yml for build instructions
CodeDeploy: automated deployment to EC2, Lambda, and ECS
CodePipeline: orchestrates the stages (source, build, test, deploy)
Artifacts are passed between stages via S3 buckets
common_misconceptions: CodePipeline can only deploy to AWS services (CodeDeploy can deploy to on-premises servers too)
CodeBuild runs indefinitely (default timeout is 60 minutes, max is 8 hours)

name: CodeDeploy Strategies
description: Deployment strategies for EC2, Lambda, and ECS using CodeDeploy
key_facts: EC2/On-premises: In-place (rolling) and Blue/Green deployment types
Lambda: Canary, Linear, and AllAtOnce traffic shifting strategies
appspec.yml defines deployment actions (hooks, files, permissions)
EC2 hooks order: BeforeInstall, AfterInstall, ApplicationStart, ValidateService
Automatic rollback on deployment failure or CloudWatch alarm triggers
common_misconceptions: In-place deployment on EC2 creates new instances (it updates existing instances; Blue/Green creates new ones)
Lambda Blue/Green deployment replaces the function code (it uses aliases to shift traffic between versions)

name: CloudFormation and SAM
description: Infrastructure as Code templates and serverless application framework
key_facts: CloudFormation templates define resources in JSON or YAML
Key sections: Parameters, Resources (required), Outputs, Mappings, Conditions
Intrinsic functions: !Ref, !GetAtt, !Sub, !Join, Fn::ImportValue for cross-stack references
SAM is a CloudFormation extension for serverless (AWS::Serverless::Function, API, SimpleTable)
sam build, sam local invoke for local testing, sam deploy for deployment
common_misconceptions: SAM replaces CloudFormation (SAM extends CloudFormation and is transformed to CloudFormation at deployment)
CloudFormation stacks can be updated without risk (updates can fail or cause resource replacement; use change sets to preview)

name: Elastic Beanstalk
description: PaaS for deploying web applications with managed infrastructure
key_facts: Supports Java, .NET, Python, Node.js, Go, Ruby, PHP, Docker
Deployment policies: All at once, Rolling, Rolling with additional batch, Immutable, Traffic splitting
Immutable deployment creates new instances in a new ASG for zero-downtime deployments
Environment configuration via .ebextensions/ YAML files in the application bundle
Worker tier processes background tasks from SQS queues
common_misconceptions: Elastic Beanstalk is only for simple applications (it supports complex multi-tier architectures with customization)
You lose control of underlying resources (you retain full access to EC2 instances, load balancers, and RDS created by Beanstalk)

name: CloudWatch Logs and Metrics
description: Application monitoring, log aggregation, and custom metrics for troubleshooting
key_facts: CloudWatch Logs collects logs from Lambda, EC2 (via agent), ECS, API Gateway, and more
Log groups organize logs by source; log streams represent individual instances or invocations
Metric filters extract custom metrics from log data (e.g., count error occurrences)
CloudWatch Alarms trigger actions based on metric thresholds (SNS, Auto Scaling, EC2 actions)
Custom metrics: use PutMetricData API to publish application-specific metrics
common_misconceptions: EC2 instances send logs to CloudWatch automatically (you must install and configure the CloudWatch agent)
CloudWatch metrics have 1-second granularity by default (standard resolution is 1 minute; high resolution is 1 second at extra cost)

name: X-Ray Tracing and Debugging
description: Distributed tracing for analyzing and debugging microservices architectures
key_facts: X-Ray SDK instruments code to capture trace data (segments and subsegments)
Annotations are indexed key-value pairs for filtering traces
Metadata is non-indexed data for additional context in traces
Service map visualizes the architecture and latency of each component
Sampling rules control which requests are traced to manage cost
common_misconceptions: Metadata and annotations serve the same purpose (annotations are indexed for search/filter, metadata is not)
X-Ray traces every request (sampling rules determine which requests are traced; default is 1 per second plus 5% of additional requests)

name: Lambda Debugging and Errors
description: Common Lambda errors, debugging techniques, and performance optimization
key_facts: Timeout errors: function exceeds configured timeout (check downstream service latency)
Out of memory: function exceeds allocated memory (increase memory allocation)
Throttling (429): concurrent execution limit reached; use reserved concurrency
Cold starts: initial invocation latency; mitigate with provisioned concurrency
Lambda Destinations route async invocation results to SQS, SNS, Lambda, or EventBridge
common_misconceptions: Cold starts affect all invocations equally (only the first invocation or after idle period experiences cold starts)
Provisioned concurrency eliminates all latency (it removes cold start latency but not function execution time)
